process_and_outcome: Formal opinions and audits resulted in approved practices with recommendations implemented, demonstrating proactive rights protection in administrative data handling.

example_name: CJEU Judgment in WS and Others v Minister for Justice (Take Back Control Directive)
example_background: A group of Pakistani nationals challenged Ireland's procedures for handling international protection applications, alleging delays and lack of effective remedies violated rights to asylum and judicial protection under EU law.
process_and_outcome: In May 2023, the CJEU ruled in Case C-840/21 that member states must ensure timely processing and effective remedies, reinforcing procedural rights standards.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: Schrems II - Facebook Data Transfers
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, challenged Facebook's transfer of personal data from EU users to servers in the United States, arguing that US surveillance laws, particularly those enabling access by intelligence agencies like the NSA, did not provide adequate protection equivalent to EU fundamental rights standards. The case built on a prior 2015 ruling (Schrems I) that invalidated the Safe Harbor framework. Following the EU-US Privacy Shield adoption, Schrems filed complaints with the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) regarding ongoing transfers. Evidence from Snowden revelations underscored bulk data access by US authorities without sufficient safeguards for non-US persons.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the CJEU invalidated the Privacy Shield decision and required stricter scrutiny of Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for transfers, emphasizing that data exporters must ensure equivalent protection in the recipient country. Facebook ceased certain transfers and adapted compliance measures.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: La Quadrature du Net - French Data Retention Laws
example_background: French privacy NGO La Quadrature du Net and others challenged national laws implementing data retention obligations for telecom operators, requiring storage of metadata for up to a year. The laws permitted generalized retention for security purposes. The case was brought before the French Conseil d'État, which referred preliminary questions to the CJEU regarding compatibility with EU law amid ongoing national security debates.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the CJEU ruled that generalized, indiscriminate retention of IP addresses, traffic, and location data is incompatible with EU law unless justified by a targeted threat to national security. France amended its laws to introduce stricter limits.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: Wabe v Stadt Frankfurt am Main
example_background: Two Muslim childcare workers at a Frankfurt day-care center run by the city were prohibited from wearing headscarves under a neutrality policy banning religious symbols during work hours to promote tolerance among children. They challenged the policy as discriminatory. Lower German courts referred questions to the CJEU on whether such policies infringe equality rights when children of various backgrounds attend.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the CJEU held that public authorities providing childcare services may impose bans on religious attire if justified by the pursuit of neutrality and provided the policy is general and undifferentiated. National courts assess proportionality.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: European Commission v Hungary - NGO Law
example_background: Hungary enacted a 2017 law requiring NGOs receiving over €24,300 from foreign sources to register as 'foreign-funded' organizations, amid government concerns over political influence. The Commission argued this stigmatized civil society and interfered with freedom of association, contrary to common constitutional traditions reflected in EU law.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the CJEU annulled key provisions of the law, finding they created unjustified restrictions on NGO operations and funding transparency requirements that disproportionately burdened fundamental freedoms.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)
